content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
April 15, 1999
Gerald James
Labor Relations Counsel
Association of California State Attorneys
and Administrative Law Judges
660 J Street, Suite 480
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-083
Dear Mr. James:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Because you have requested general information, we can only
provide informal assistance in response to your questions. In addition, the provision of informal
assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
QUESTION
Are members of the board for the Association of California State Attorneys and
Administrative Law Judges (ACSA) required to report travel reimbursement or direct payments
associated with performance of their duties as ACSA board members on their Statements of
Economic Interest?
CONCLUSION
The travel payments you mention are reportable by the board members on their
Statements of Economic Interest as income, assuming the board members provide ACSA with
equal or greater consideration for the payments.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


April 15, 1999
Page Two
1-99-083
FACTS
ACSA is a not-for-profit corporation organized under IRS Code section 503(c)(5).
ACSA is the bargaining representative for state bargaining unit 2 for state legal professionals.
All board members for ACSA are volunteers and state employees. Board members are
reimbursed for ordinary actual expenses associated with their duties as ACSA board members,
including transportation and related lodging and subsistence. You sought telephone advice from
the Commission in early 1997 and were told that such “travel payments” were not reportable.
ANALYSIS
Section 82030 defines income as:
“A payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance,
reimbursementfor expenses, per diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension
program ....“ (Emphasis added.)
In subsection (b), section 82030 provides exceptions to this definition, including:
“Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state, local,
or federal government agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per
diem from a bonafide nonprofit entity exemptfrom taxation under Section
501(c)(3) ofthe Internal Revenue Code.” (Emphasis added.)’
The advice you received in January 1997 was correct at that time. However, Section
82030(b)(2) was amended in 1997 to include reimbursements only from 501(c)(3) organizations.
Since ACSA is a 503(c)(5) organization, reimbursements are now reportable as income,
assuming that your board members provide ACSA equal or greater consideration for the
payments. If this is not the case, the payments are considered gifts, and reportable and limited to
$300 per year. (Section 82028.)2
2 Your analysis of our publication on gifts, honoraria, and travel is incorrect. Section UI, subdivision 7
states that certain types of travel payments are not prohibited, limited, nor reportable. However, number 7 refers
only to the items specifically listed in the publication as exempt from the definition of “gift”; in this context, they
are not reportable. The travel payments from your organization in question are not included in the specified items,


If you have any additional questions, please do not hesitate to call me.
Sincerely,
April 15, 1999
Page Three
1-99-083
Steven G. Churchwell
General
Reform Consultant

Economic Interest?

Page Two
1-99-083

QUESTION: Are members of the board for the Association of California State Attorneys and
Administrative Law Judges (ACSA) required to report travel reimbursement or direct payments
associated with performance of their duties as ACSA board members on their Statements of
Economic Interest?

CONCLUSION: The travel payments you mention are reportable by the board members on their
Statements of Economic Interest as income, assuming the board members provide ACSA with
equal or greater consideration for the payments.April 15, 1999
Page Two
1-99-083
